items.snippet: Runtime & HTTP Tokio - async runtime, the concurrency foundation. async-std was skipped due to weaker ecosystem support. Axum - HTTP routing with strong type safety, sits naturally on Tower. actix-web was ruled out for being more actor-oriented and less aligned here. Tower / tower-http / tower_governor - middleware as composable layers: CORS, tracing, request IDs, and rate limiting all live here, outside business logic where they belong.

items.snippet: Data & messaging SQLx - async PostgreSQL with compile-time query validation. Diesel's DSL felt too restrictive for the fine-tuned SQL this system needs. Lapin - AMQP client for RabbitMQ. Chosen for fine-grained control; higher-level wrappers hide too much. Redis - idempotency checks and distributed locks. Fast coordination layer between workers and the API.

items.snippet: Serialization & validation Serde + serde_json - standard, mature, no real alternative in Rust. Validator - declarative rules on DTOs, enforced before any core logic runs. phonenumber - international phone validation. Regex simply isn't reliable for global numbering rules. UUID + Chrono - non-sequential IDs and consistent UTC timestamps throughout.

items.snippet: Tokio as the runtime - why Async-first ecosystem alignment. Every library in this stack - sqlx , lapin , redis , axum - is built on Tokio. They share the same async primitives, the same executor, and the same task model. No bridging, no compatibility shims, no runtime conflicts. Non-blocking async I/O. Tokio multiplexes thousands of concurrent connections - database queries, RabbitMQ publishes, HTTP requests - across a small fixed thread pool. No thread-per-request model, no OS thread overhead. The workers and API can handle massive concurrency without proportional memory cost. select! and CancellationToken for shutdown. Graceful shutdown across every worker loop is built around tokio::selec…
items.searchText: application stack core tooling and libraries for the verification platform. application stack tokio as the runtime - why  tokio as the runtime - why tokio as the runtime - why async-first ecosystem alignment. every library in this stack - sqlx , lapin , redis , axum - is built on tokio. they share the same async primitives, the same executor, and the same task model. no bridging, no compatibility shims, no runtime conflicts. non-blocking async i/o. tokio multiplexes thousands of concurrent connections - database queries, rabbitmq publishes, http requests - across a small fixed thread pool. no thread-per-request model, no os thread overhead. the workers and api can handle massive concurrency without proportional memory cost. select! and cancellationtoken for shutdown. graceful shutdown across every worker loop is built around tokio::select! and cancellationtoken . these are tokio primitives - clean, composable, and cooperative with the async runtime rather than fighting it. joinset for bounded concurrency. the stage processor runs multiple jobs concurrently inside a joinset . this gives structured concurrency - tasks are tracked, drained on shutdown, and never leak - which is not possible with raw spawn calls. time control in tests. tokio::time::pause() and tokio::time::advance() let the lease recovery tests simulate a 60-second polling loop in milliseconds. no real waiting, no slow ci. this is only possible because tokio owns the time abstraction inside the async runtime.

This means middleware - rate limiting, tracing, CORS, request IDs - is composed as standard Tower layers, completely outside business logic. The same middleware primitives work across the entire HTTP stack without framework-specific glue. Compile-time extractor safety. items.searchText: application stack core tooling and libraries for the verification platform. application stack axum + tower - why  axum + tower - why axum + tower - why native tokio integration. axum is built by the tokio team and runs directly on tokio’s runtime. no adapter layers, no compatibility shims - it shares the same async executor as every other dependency in the stack. every route is a tower::service . this means middleware - rate limiting, tracing, cors, request ids - is composed as standard tower layers, completely outside business logic. the same middleware primitives work across the entire http stack without framework-specific glue. compile-time extractor safety. fromrequest and fromrequestparts enforce validation and authentication at the type level. a handler that requires validatedjson<t> or validatedclient literally cannot compile without those extractors being correctly wired. errors are caught before the binary runs, not at request time. zero framework overhead. axum adds no reflection, no runtime magic, no macro-heavy annotation scanning. routing is a pointer lookup. the abstraction cost is zero - raw hyper performance with structured routing on top.

items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture high-level architecture  high-level architecture high-level architecture preview - opens full diagram in google drive click to view architecture diagram (google drive) three workers. one clear job each before anything else - this system runs exactly three background workers, and none of them overlap in responsibility: outbox relay bridges postgres to rabbitmq stage processor consumes from rabbitmq and drives verification logic lease recovery cleans up crashed jobs, runs entirely out-of-band that's the foundation. every data path in this system flows through one of those three, or the api itself. the topology edge → api layer the client hits the api over http/rest. the api talks to exactly two things: redis to validate the idempotency key, and postgres to write the session and seed the outbox_events table transactionally. the api never touches rabbitmq - that boundary is intentional. redis exclusive to the api layer. workers never interact with it. postgresql the central nervous system. four things connect to it: the api (writes sessions and outbox rows); the outbox relay worker (reads and deletes outbox rows); the stage processor worker (updates stage statuses, inserts next outbox row on stage unlock); the lease recovery worker (scans for zombies, resets them, re-inserts outbox rows). outbox relay worker polls outbox_events from postgres via its own isolated connection pool, then pushes those events into rabbitmq over tcp. it's the only component that writes to the broker. rabbitmq sits entirely between the workers. receives from the outbox relay, delivers to the stage processor. holds the main, retry, and dead-letter queues. stage processor worker consumes from rabbitmq with a strict prefetch of 1. updates stage status in postgres. if a completed stage unlocks the next one, it writes the successor's outbox_event directly back into postgres - feeding the relay again. lease recovery worker postgres only. no rabbitmq. scans for timed-out or crashed jobs, resets their state, and inserts fresh outbox_events - which naturally flow back through the outbox relay as if nothing crashed.

items.snippet: API architecture Foundation: one process, many concurrent requests Before diving into the layers - the API is a single OS process running a Tokio async runtime. There are no threads per request, no forking, no copying of state. Tokio multiplexes thousands of concurrent connections across a small fixed thread pool. Everything below is built on top of that single fact. The HTTP engine - Hyper & Axum Axum is not a heavy framework. It's a thin routing layer on top of Hyper, which handles the raw TCP byte stream and HTTP header parsing. Every route is a tower::Service - a typed async function from Request to Response. Routing a request to a handler is a pointer lookup, nothing more. No reflection…
items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture api architecture  api architecture api architecture foundation: one process, many concurrent requests before diving into the layers - the api is a single os process running a tokio async runtime. there are no threads per request, no forking, no copying of state. tokio multiplexes thousands of concurrent connections across a small fixed thread pool. everything below is built on top of that single fact. the http engine - hyper & axum axum is not a heavy framework. it's a thin routing layer on top of hyper, which handles the raw tcp byte stream and http header parsing. every route is a tower::service - a typed async function from request to response. routing a request to a handler is a pointer lookup, nothing more. no reflection, no magic, no overhead. shared memory - arc<appstate> the postgres pool, redis manager, amqp connection, and config all live in one appstate struct, wrapped in an arc. ten thousand concurrent requests don't get ten thousand copies - they get ten thousand pointers to the same struct, backed by a single atomic counter increment. one allocation at boot, zero copies at runtime. the interceptors - fromrequest validation never lives inside handlers. validatedjson<t> implements fromrequest, so axum runs it before the handler function is even scheduled. if serde parsing or field validation fails, the extractor returns a 400 or 422 directly and the handler never wakes up. the edge is the enforcement point. the middleware onion - tower layers every request passes through a stack of tower::layer s before it reaches the router. the outermost layer is tracelayer - it creates a tracing span and stamps a trace_id onto the async context. every db query, redis call, and log line emitted deeper in the stack inherits that id automatically. no manual propagation needed. the database multiplexer - pgpool postgres connections are opened once at boot, held in pgpool, and leased per request via pool.begin().await . when the transaction commits or the variable drops out of scope, the connection returns to the pool immediately. this is what lets 20 postgres connections serve thousands of concurrent http requests without contention.

Request contract - zero trust at the border The principle: assume every incoming request is malformed or malicious until the type system proves otherwise. Business logic never sees bad data. ValidatedJson<T> runs a two-pass check before the handler wakes up. The first pass is structura…
items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture api architecture request contract request contract request contract url design - resource-oriented slugs post /v1/verify/{session_id}/stages/{stage_name} every segment is deliberate. /v1/ is explicit versioning - when a v2 flow ships with 10 stages, both versions run concurrently without breaking existing clients. {session_id} in the path rather than the body lets the router and log infrastructure (cloudwatch) index by session id without parsing json payloads. request contract - zero trust at the border the principle: assume every incoming request is malformed or malicious until the type system proves otherwise. business logic never sees bad data. validatedjson<t> runs a two-pass check before the handler wakes up. the first pass is structural - does the json deserialize into the expected rust struct? the second pass is semantic - is that phone number actually valid for nigeria or the uk? both passes must succeed. failure at either returns a 400 or 422 immediately at the extractor layer. x-api-key authentication uses the subtle crate for constant-time comparison. standard string equality ( == ) short-circuits and returns faster when early characters match - an attacker can measure response times and progressively guess the key character by character. constant-time comparison takes identical time regardless of how many characters match, eliminating that attack surface entirely. idempotency-key is optional on session start. once present, it gates duplicate submissions at the redis layer before anything touches the database.

When a frontend engineer reports a 500, you don't ask for a screenshot - you ask for the trace_id . Paste it into your logs and you get the exact database queries, Redis checks, and RabbitMQ interactions for that specific request, down to t…
items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture api architecture response contract response contract response contract predictable by design every response from this api, success or failure, follows a fixed shape. clients never have to guess what they'll get back. success (201/202) { "session_id": "uuid", "status": "pending", "message": "human readable hint" } error (4xx/5xx) { "error": "validation_failed", "message": "one or more fields failed validation", "trace_id": "016x_hex_string", "details": { "phone": "invalid format" } } trace_id on every error. when a frontend engineer reports a 500, you don't ask for a screenshot - you ask for the trace_id . paste it into your logs and you get the exact database queries, redis checks, and rabbitmq interactions for that specific request, down to the millisecond. no guessing, no reproduction steps needed. 202 accepted instead of 200 ok . stage submissions return 202 because 200 implies the work is done. it isn't - the kyc processing happens asynchronously via rabbitmq workers. 202 is an explicit contract with the client: "we received it, validated it, and queued it. the engine is still running." error masking on 500s. the real error - relation "users" does not exist , a raw postgres panic - is logged internally. the client only sees "an unexpected error occurred" . exposing internal error messages leaks your database schema, table names, and system internals to anyone probing the api.

items.snippet: Worker architecture Foundation: workers are not threads Before the layers - each worker is a single long-running Tokio async task, not a thread pool spinning up per job. There's no thread-per-message model here. Tokio's event loop cooperatively schedules everything, and the primitives below are what make that safe, concurrent, and survivable under real infrastructure pressure. The async engine - Tokio & JoinSets Each worker runs an infinite loop {} inside a Tokio task. When the Stage Processor pulls multiple messages, it doesn't process them one-by-one, nor does it blindly tokio::spawn (which leaks if tasks hang). Every message becomes a future pushed into a tokio::task::JoinSet . The set fi…
items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture worker architecture  worker architecture worker architecture foundation: workers are not threads before the layers - each worker is a single long-running tokio async task, not a thread pool spinning up per job. there's no thread-per-message model here. tokio's event loop cooperatively schedules everything, and the primitives below are what make that safe, concurrent, and survivable under real infrastructure pressure. the async engine - tokio & joinsets each worker runs an infinite loop {} inside a tokio task. when the stage processor pulls multiple messages, it doesn't process them one-by-one, nor does it blindly tokio::spawn (which leaks if tasks hang). every message becomes a future pushed into a tokio::task::joinset . the set fires them concurrently - the moment one task awaits network i/o, the cpu is yielded to another. parallelism without threads. graceful shutdown - cancellationtoken kubernetes doesn't ask nicely - it sends sigterm and counts down. every loop and background task gets a clone of a tokio_util::sync::cancellationtoken at boot. when shutdown fires, cancel.cancel() triggers. every select! branch listening on that token stops pulling new messages from rabbitmq. the worker then drains its joinset , waiting for in-flight tasks to finish writing to postgres before the process exits. no mid-transaction kills. the data backbone - sqlx transaction boundaries no orm. every worker operation that touches postgres - claim a job, unlock a successor, sweep a session - opens one pool.begin().await and passes &mut tx through every call. either everything commits, or the connection drops and the entire block rolls back atomically. there's no partial state possible. messaging - lapin & explicit acks a single lapin::connection over tcp multiplexes lightweight virtual channel s. workers consume with manual acknowledgement - the delivery object is held in memory until the sqlx transaction commits successfully. only then does delivery.basic_ack() fire. if the payload is malformed, basic_reject(requeue: false) severs the message and routes it to the dead-letter queue. nothing is auto-acked, nothing silently disappears. time control - timeouts & intervals distributed systems hang before they crash. every external call is wrapped in tokio::time::timeout - rabbitmq publishes get a hard 50ms ceiling; if the tcp socket silently drops packets, the future is aborted and an error is returned rather than a hung thread. polling workers use tokio::time::interval() with missedtickbehavior::delay - if a postgres fetch runs long under load, the worker waits a full fresh interval before querying again. it doesn't try to catch up. that's the pressure release valve.

dockerfile.test │ └── dockerfile.worker └── scripts/ └── verification-stress.mjs thought process one rule - keep business logic in one place the entire structure is driven by a single architectural decision: crates/core owns everything, apps/ owns nothing. the two binaries (api and worker) are deliberately kept dumb - they boot tokio, wire infrastructure, and delegate. all business rules, sql, validation, and worker logic live in core. this means you can test the full domain without spinning up an http server. you can scale the api and workers independently as separate os processes without duplicating a single line of logic. root level the project/ folder is the cargo workspace root. cargo.toml pins all shared dependency versions once - individual crates just inherit them. docker-compose.yml and makefile live here so every make and docker compose command resolves paths correctly from this single working directory. apps/ - the two thin binaries these are the deployable processes. neither contains business logic. apps/api/ - boots tokio, constructs arc<appstate> , mounts the axum router from core, and listens on the configured port. app.rs wires the tower middleware stack; router.rs maps url paths to handlers defined in core. apps/worker/ - boots tokio, constructs arc<appstate> , and starts the three background workers registered in registry.rs. nothing else. crates/core/src/ - the brain everything domain-critical lives here, organized by concern. shared/ cross-cutting infrastructure. config.rs - typed appconfig from env via envy. state.rs - arc<appstate> with pool, redis, amqp, config. error.rs - central apperror; all errors convert here before http responses. extract.rs - validatedjson extractor. redis.rs - redis wiring and idempotency helpers. tracing.rs - subscriber init; json in prod, pretty locally. verification/ vertical slice. http.rs - axum handlers and dtos (edge only). service.rs - orchestration; db + messaging; business rules. db.rs - raw sqlx on &pgpool or &mut transaction . types.rs - verificationsession, stagestatus, etc. helpers.rs - pure in-slice functions. openapi.rs - utoipa schemas for compile-time openapi. messaging/ rabbitmq isolated; slices call publish_stage_event(...) etc. - no scattered routing keys. topology.rs - exchanges and queues in one place. publisher.rs - typed publish for service and workers. workers/ outbox_relay.rs - poll outbox_events , publish to rabbitmq. stage_processor.rs - consume, drive stage transitions in postgres. stage_lease_recovery.rs - zombie jobs, out-of-band reset. jitter.rs - shared retry jitter. crates/core/tests/ unit/ - pure logic: config, errors, extractor, types - no i/o. integration/ - e2e against real postgres, redis, and rabbitmq; one file per slice. support/ - harnesses and fixtures shared by integration tests. migrations/ ordered, append-only. applied once by sqlx migrate run . sequence: sessions → stages → outbox → lease → recovery index. never edited after apply. devops/ one dockerfile per process (api, worker, migrations, test). each image purpose-built and minimal. scripts/ verification-stress.mjs - parallel sessions, csv reports; tooling, not app code.

items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture data architecture  data architecture data architecture preview - opens full diagram in google drive click to view erd diagram (google drive) verification_sessions table tracks the top-level lifecycle of a verification attempt. uuid primary key prevents enumeration. status is the terminal state machine: active, completed, or failed. completed_at and failed_at are separate nullable columns - not a single ended_at - because how it ended matters. updated_at is trigger-managed, always accurate. idx_sessions_status covers any sweep filtering by status. verification_stages table one row per verification step (1-5) within a session. on delete cascade means session deletion cleans up all stages automatically. stage is a smallint - smaller, faster index lookups. status follows locked → pending → processing → completed | failed. input_data is written once by the api, never touched again. response_data is written only by the worker on completion. two writers, two columns - the audit trail is structurally immutable. retry_count and error track failure history; error is cleared on a successful retry. expires_at turns a job claim into a timed lease. a crashed worker pod can't leave a stage stuck in processing forever - the timestamp expires and the recovery worker reclaims it. lease_recovery_count prevents infinite recovery loops on top of that - a toxic payload that crashes the worker every time would be reset to pending forever without it. once it hits the configured limit, the stage is permanently failed and abandoned. uq_session_stage makes duplicate stage rows physically impossible. chk_valid_stage rejects out-of-range values at the db level regardless of what the application sends. three indexes: idx_stages_polling - partial on pending/processing only, so worker polling never scans completed rows. idx_stages_session - covers the sweep query reading all stages for a given session. idx_stages_processing_expires_at - partial on processing rows with a non-null expires_at , so expired lease lookups by the recovery worker are always an index scan, never a table scan. outbox_events table the transactional outbox bridge between postgres and rabbitmq. stores exchange, routing_key, and payload (jsonb including the worker's idempotency event_id ). rows are hard-deleted after the relay gets an amqp ack - never updated, not an audit log, designed to stay near-empty. idx_outbox_polling on created_at asc keeps oldest-first polling instant even during traffic spikes. uuidv4 over bigserial - why sequential integers are fine for simple crud apps. for a distributed kyc engine handling sensitive identity data, they're a security flaw. idor immunity. with id = 100, a malicious script trivially guesses id = 101 and scrapes other users' verification states. uuidv4 carries 122 bits of entropy - guessing one is not computationally feasible. the id itself is a security layer. bearer token model. the verification tables store no user_id - the session uuid is the credential. the client holds it, presents it, and the engine trusts it. for a url parameter to function as a bearer token, it must be unguessable by definition. no database round-trips for id generation. sequential ids require a round-trip to claim the next value from a locked database sequence - a concurrency bottleneck at high throughput. uuids are generated entirely in memory: uuid::new_v4() in rust, inline, with no db interaction. multi-stage transactions don't stall waiting for an id to come back. distributed uniqueness. the transactional outbox relays events across workers and pods. sequential ids from two database shards would collide - both would produce id = 5. a uuidv4 generated in pod a will never collide with one generated in pod b, ever. the trade-off. 16 bytes versus 8 bytes for a bigint, and b-tree indexes on random uuids fragment slightly more. that cost is accepted - a few extra megabytes of storage in exchange for idor immunity, stateless id generation, and collision-free distributed operation.

Our entire at-least-once delivery guarantee depends on explicit ACK/NACK control. Dead-letter topology. items.searchText: architecture topology, api and worker design, contracts, repository layout, data model, and messaging. architecture message queue architecture  message queue architecture message queue architecture firstly, why rabbitmq instead of redis? rabbitmq over redis for messaging - why persistence. messages are written to disk with delivery mode 2. a broker restart does not lose in-flight jobs. redis pub/sub is fire-and-forget - if no subscriber is listening at that exact moment, the message is gone. explicit acknowledgement. rabbitmq holds a message until the consumer explicitly acks it. redis has no concept of this - once delivered, it’s delivered. our entire at-least-once delivery guarantee depends on explicit ack/nack control. dead-letter topology. the three-tier architecture - main queue, wait queue, terminal graveyard - is native rabbitmq infrastructure. ttl-based retry routing back to the main queue is built into the broker. replicating this in redis requires custom application code for every failure scenario. consumer group semantics. rabbitmq distributes messages across competing consumers automatically with skip locked-equivalent behavior at the broker level. redis streams can do this too, but it’s significantly more complex to operate correctly. backpressure control. basic_qos(prefetch=1) tells the broker exactly how many unacknowledged messages to push to a consumer at once. redis has no equivalent - the consumer controls its own polling rate, which pushes backpressure management entirely into application code. operational visibility. the rabbitmq management ui shows queue depths, consumer counts, message rates, and dead-letter contents in real time. debugging a stuck job means opening a browser tab, not writing redis commands. foundation: three tiers, one rule - no message is ever lost preview - opens full diagram in google drive click to view queue topology diagram (google drive) every message in this system has exactly three possible fates: it succeeds, it retries, or it lands in a graveyard for human inspection. there is no fourth option where it silently disappears. the publishers messages are published with delivery mode 2 - persistent. rabbitmq writes them to disk before acknowledging. a broker crash or restart does not lose in-flight messages. publishing uses a double-await pattern. the first await sends the bytes over tcp. the second await blocks until rabbitmq sends back an explicit confirmation that the message has been safely received. no fire-and-forget, no silent data loss. when a failed message is moved to the retry queue, the existing x-death headers are preserved and carried forward. these headers track how many times a message has failed across the entire system - without them, the worker has no way to enforce a max-retry limit. the payload itself is kept deliberately minimal - just uuids and a stage number. no pii, no full database rows. if the worker needs more context, it queries postgres using those ids. this keeps queues lightweight and eliminates the class of bugs where a worker acts on stale data embedded in a message. the three-tier topology the topology is declared idempotently on every boot. if the exchanges and queues already exist, rabbitmq ignores the declarations. if they don't, it creates them. the infrastructure heals itself automatically - no manual setup required after a fresh environment. all exchanges use direct routing - one message, one consumer. no fanout, no broadcast. tier 1 - main queue where active jobs live. workers consume from here. when a worker hits max retries and calls basic_reject(requeue=false) , rabbitmq doesn't drop the message - the x-dead-letter-exchange configuration automatically routes it to the terminal exchange instead. tier 2 - wait queue the sleep queue. no workers consume from it. failed messages that haven't yet hit the retry limit land here and sit for exactly 30 seconds via x-message-ttl . when the ttl expires, rabbitmq itself routes the message back to the main queue using the configured dead-letter exchange and routing key. fully infrastructure-driven retry backoff - no sleep timers, no scheduled tasks, no rust code involved. tier 3 - dead queue the graveyard. no ttl, no workers, no automatic routing. poison messages - ones that crash the worker every time, or ones rejected after hitting the retry ceiling - land here permanently. a human engineer can inspect them in the rabbitmq management ui, diagnose the root cause, fix the bug, and manually shovel them back to the main queue when ready.

items.snippet: Runtime ENVIRONMENT - Switches log format. local gives readable pretty logs; anything else outputs compact JSON for log pipelines. Don't run local in production - it's verbose and expensive to ingest. SERVER_PORT - TCP port the API binds to. Exists so infrastructure can remap ports without touching code. RATE_LIMIT_REQUESTS_PER_MIN - Per-IP request cap enforced in memory. Increase during expected traffic spikes to reduce false 429s. Decrease during an active attack to throttle an abusive IP faster. CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS - Empty disables CORS entirely. Populated values are parsed at boot - a typo crashes the app immediately rather than silently breaking browser traffic in production.

items.snippet: PostgreSQL - Main Pool DB_POOL_MIN / MAX - Min keeps warm connections ready; max caps total connections from this process. With 50 API pods at max 20, you're using 1,000 connections - that hits Postgres's global limit fast. Increase max for more parallelism; Decrease if running many replicas against one DB. DB_POOL_ACQUIRE_MS - How long a request waits for a free connection before returning a 500. Too high and requests pile up in RAM until the pod OOMs. Too low and you get premature 500s under short spikes. DB_POOL_IDLE_MS - How long idle connections are kept before being closed. Increase to reduce reconnect churn; Decrease to release Postgres sessions faster on quiet nodes.

items.searchText: devops build strategy, deployment safety, migrations, and test image workflow. devops api & worker images  api & worker images api & worker images both use a multi-stage cargo-chef build. chef separates dependency compilation from application compilation into distinct docker layers. the first build is slow - all 200+ dependencies compile from scratch. every subsequent build skips that layer entirely and only recompiles changed application code. a code change that previously took 10 minutes now takes roughly 10 seconds. the build base is pinned to bookworm to match the runtime image ( distroless/cc-debian12 ). mismatched glibc versions between build and runtime cause immediate crashes - a binary compiled against a newer glibc simply won't run in the older distroless container. the versions are locked together deliberately. sqlx_offline=true means sql queries are verified against committed json metadata instead of a live database. ci pipelines don't need a running postgres instance to compile - no flaky pipeline failures from missing database connections. the final images are distroless - no shell, no package manager, no system utilities, roughly 50mb. if a zero-day is exploited, there are no tools available inside the container to do anything with it. api and worker are separate images built from the same source. they scale independently in kubernetes - more worker pods for processing backlogs, without spinning up unnecessary api servers alongside them.

items.snippet: Migrations image Migrations run in a dedicated ephemeral container, not inside the API startup code. Ten API pods booting simultaneously and all trying to migrate would deadlock each other. The migration container runs first, exits with code 0, and only then does the API rollout begin. sqlx-cli is compiled against musl and statically linked, then dropped into a plain Alpine image. No dynamic library dependencies. ca-certificates is installed explicitly - without it, the binary can't verify SSL certificates against managed cloud databases like RDS or Supabase. Only the Postgres feature is compiled in - no MySQL or SQLite drivers bloating the binary. Final image is around 30MB.

items.snippet: API execution Firstly, why does the verification engine need a controlled API entry point? The verification engine needs a controlled, secure entry point. Without one, clients have no way to interact with the system - there’s no way to start a session, submit data, or check progress. But the API’s role is more specific than just "accept requests." It exists to be the single point where untrusted external input is transformed into trusted internal state. Everything the API does is about that boundary - validating that the data is correct, authenticating that the caller is allowed, preventing duplicates, and writing the result into Postgres atomically. By the time data leaves the API layer, it…
items.searchText: execution how the api and workers run: request flow, worker behavior, and end-to-end phases. execution api execution  api execution api execution firstly, why does the verification engine need a controlled api entry point? the verification engine needs a controlled, secure entry point. without one, clients have no way to interact with the system - there’s no way to start a session, submit data, or check progress. but the api’s role is more specific than just "accept requests." it exists to be the single point where untrusted external input is transformed into trusted internal state. everything the api does is about that boundary - validating that the data is correct, authenticating that the caller is allowed, preventing duplicates, and writing the result into postgres atomically. by the time data leaves the api layer, it has been fully vetted and safely persisted. the workers downstream can trust everything they receive from the database because the api already rejected anything that didn’t belong there. it also exists to enforce the contract with clients. the workers don’t speak http. they have no concept of a request or a response. the api translates between the http world the client lives in and the async, queue-driven world the workers operate in. it accepts a synchronous http call, does its work, and returns 202 accepted - explicitly telling the client "your data is safe, but the result will come later." that translation is the api’s core purpose. without it, there’s no authentication, no validation, no idempotency, no structured response contract, and no clean separation between what the client does and what the workers do. the entire system’s reliability depends on the entry point being strict. the api is that strictness, codified. the api layer - what it does and how it flows the api is a gateway. it validates, locks data into postgres, and hands off to workers. it does no kyc work itself. 1. every request passes through security before routing the x-api-key header is checked using constant-time comparison - no timing attacks. if the request carries an idempotency-key , redis is checked first. a duplicate request never reaches postgres - the cached response is returned immediately. rate limiting is enforced in memory before any database or business logic is touched. 2. all incoming json goes through a two-pass validation serde validates structure first - wrong types are rejected immediately. the validator crate then enforces field rules: string lengths, enum bounds, required formats. phone numbers go through libphonenumber for e.164 verification. any failure returns a 422 with exact field-level errors. the handler function never runs on invalid input. 3. stage submission opens one transaction and does exactly three things first, it updates the stage from locked to pending . if that update affects 0 rows - because the stage is already in progress or completed - the api returns 409 conflict and stops. no duplicate work enters the system. second, it evaluates the dependency rule. independent stages (phone, liveness, face-match) get an outbox_events row inserted immediately. dependent stages (personal info, document) only get the outbox row if their predecessor is already completed - otherwise the insert is skipped and the worker handles the trigger later. third, the transaction commits and the api returns 202 accepted . the work is queued; the background engine takes over from here. 4. the pool has a hard acquire timeout if the database is under load, the api waits a maximum of 10 seconds for a connection. after that it returns a 500 and moves on. without this, requests pile up in memory until the pod crashes. 5. status polling is a single endpoint one get /status call returns the parent session and all five stage rows in one indexed query. the response maps input_data and response_data together into an ordered timeline - the frontend sees the full verification journey in one shot. 6. errors always return the same shell every 4xx and 5xx includes a trace_id tied to that specific request's tracing span. a client reports a bug, hands over the trace_id , and the exact database queries and validation errors for that millisecond are immediately retrievable from logs. api documentation api documentation is generated at compile time from the rust structs and route macros directly. the swagger ui at /swagger-ui always reflects the actual code. global headers (every request) x-api-key - required on all endpoints. missing or invalid returns 401 immediately. idempotency-key - optional, used on session start. if the key exists in redis, the cached 201 response is returned without touching postgres. post /v1/verify/session/start no payload. creates one session row and five locked stage rows in a single transaction. returns 201 with the new session_id , status active , and a message. nothing is processed here - this just provisions the state machine. stage submissions - shared contract all five share the same behavior: validate the payload, lock the stage to pending , insert an outbox event, return 202 accepted . the 202 is intentional - the work is queued, not complete. post .../stages/phone - { phone_number } . validated against e.164 format via libphonenumber . post .../stages/personal-info - { first_name, last_name, dob, address } . dob is parsed as yyyy-mm-dd . string lengths are bounded. post .../stages/document - { document_type, document_image_b64, document_number } . document_type must be exactly passport , national_id , or drivers_license . anything else is a 400 . post .../stages/liveness - { liveness_image_b64 } . post .../stages/face-match - { face_image_b64 } . shared failure responses: 409 conflict - stage is not locked . 422 unprocessable entity - payload is structurally valid but fails business validation. get /v1/verify/{session_id}/status no payload. returns the full verification journey in one response - session status ( active , completed , failed ) plus all five stages in order. each stage includes its current status, the original input_data , the worker's response_data on success, and error_message on failure. error shell - always this shape { "error": "machine_readable_code", "message": "human readable hint", "details": { "field": "reason" }, "trace_id": "016x_hex_string" } the trace_id maps directly to the tracing span for that request. a reported 500 becomes a log lookup, not a debugging session.

items.snippet: Outbox relay worker Firstly, why do we have an Outbox Relay architecture? The API deliberately never talks to RabbitMQ directly. When a stage is submitted, the API opens one Postgres transaction, writes the session data, and exits. If the API also tried to publish to RabbitMQ inside that same flow, you have a dual-write problem - the database write and the RabbitMQ publish are two separate operations with no shared atomicity. The database could commit but RabbitMQ could be down . Or RabbitMQ could receive the message but the database transaction rolls back . Either way, the two systems fall out of sync and a job is either lost or processed against data that was never actually saved . The out…
items.searchText: execution how the api and workers run: request flow, worker behavior, and end-to-end phases. execution outbox relay worker  outbox relay worker outbox relay worker firstly, why do we have an outbox relay architecture? the api deliberately never talks to rabbitmq directly. when a stage is submitted, the api opens one postgres transaction, writes the session data, and exits. if the api also tried to publish to rabbitmq inside that same flow, you have a dual-write problem - the database write and the rabbitmq publish are two separate operations with no shared atomicity. the database could commit but rabbitmq could be down . or rabbitmq could receive the message but the database transaction rolls back . either way, the two systems fall out of sync and a job is either lost or processed against data that was never actually saved . the outbox table solves this by making the rabbitmq intent part of the database transaction itself. the api writes the message payload into outbox_events in the exact same commit that writes the stage row. if the transaction commits, the event is guaranteed to exist. if it rolls back, the event disappears with it. at that point, rabbitmq being up or down is completely irrelevant to the api. the outbox relay exists as a separate worker because someone has to bridge that gap - read the committed rows from postgres and actually deliver them to rabbitmq. that’s its entire job. nothing more. what it does and how it flows single mission: move rows from outbox_events into rabbitmq without losing a single one. isolated connection pool the relay uses its own dedicated postgres pool, separate from the main application pool. a rabbitmq outage that causes connections to hang can only exhaust the relay's pool - the api and stage processors keep running on the main pool, unaffected. polling with select for update skip locked the worker polls outbox_events in batches of 50 (configurable). skip locked means multiple relay pods can poll the same table simultaneously without colliding - any row locked by one pod is instantly skipped by the others. pods never wait on each other. publish with a hard 50ms timeout while publishing, the worker is holding row locks in postgres. if the rabbitmq connection hangs, waiting indefinitely would freeze those locks. every publish attempt has a strict 50ms timeout - if it expires, the row is skipped and the lock is released immediately. database health is prioritized over message dispatch speed. the batch cycle is one atomic transaction fetch and lock up to 50 rows (configurable). publish each to rabbitmq. collect the ids of successfully acknowledged messages. bulk delete only those successful rows. commit. if the pod loses power after rabbitmq acknowledges but before the delete commits, the transaction rolls back, the locks drop, and another pod picks up the same rows. at-least-once delivery, zero data loss. corrupt rows are skipped, not fatal a malformed row causes the relay to log the error, skip that row, and continue publishing the rest of the batch. one bad row never blocks the queue. smart polling cadence if the outbox is empty, the worker sleeps for a full idle interval before polling again - no pointless database hammering. on startup, a random jitter (0-5 seconds) staggers the first poll across pods, preventing a thundering herd against postgres when a deployment rolls out.

items.snippet: Lease recovery worker Firstly, why do we need a Lease Recovery Worker? The Stage Processor claims a job by writing PROCESSING to the database, then does the work. That claim has no automatic expiry - once it’s written, Postgres holds that status until something explicitly changes it. The problem is pods die. A Kubernetes node loses power, a container runs out of memory, a network partition isolates the pod - the Stage Processor is gone, but the database row still says PROCESSING . The RabbitMQ message was already ACKed when the job was claimed, so the broker has no copy to retry. The job is permanently stuck. No worker will ever pick it up again because nothing is polling for PROCESSING rows…
items.searchText: execution how the api and workers run: request flow, worker behavior, and end-to-end phases. execution lease recovery worker  lease recovery worker lease recovery worker firstly, why do we need a lease recovery worker? the stage processor claims a job by writing processing to the database, then does the work. that claim has no automatic expiry - once it’s written, postgres holds that status until something explicitly changes it. the problem is pods die. a kubernetes node loses power, a container runs out of memory, a network partition isolates the pod - the stage processor is gone, but the database row still says processing . the rabbitmq message was already acked when the job was claimed, so the broker has no copy to retry. the job is permanently stuck. no worker will ever pick it up again because nothing is polling for processing rows - only pending ones enter the queue. the expires_at column turns the claim into a timed lease. when a worker claims a job, it writes a deadline. if the worker finishes, it updates the stage and the lease becomes irrelevant. if the worker dies, the lease expires and the row sits there with status = processing and expires_at in the past - a detectable, recoverable state. the lease recovery worker exists as the only process that watches for that exact condition. it scans for expired leases, resets the stage back to pending , and inserts a fresh outbox event so the job re-enters the pipeline. without it, every crashed pod means a permanently lost job. with it, the system heals itself automatically on the next recovery tick. what it does and how it flows single mission: find jobs that were claimed but never finished, and either resurrect them or permanently bury them. search and rescue - every 60 seconds (configurable) the worker polls for stages where status = processing and expires_at is in the past. a worker that claimed a job sets a 120-second lease - if that time has passed, the pod is definitively dead. rows are fetched in batches of 25 using select for update skip locked . small batches mean a mass node failure dropping thousands of jobs doesn't lock the entire table at once - recovery happens in surgical chunks. reclaim - reset and re-queue for each expired row, the worker resets the status back to pending , increments lease_recovery_count , and inserts a new row into outbox_events - all in the same transaction. the outbox relay picks up that event and pushes the job back into rabbitmq for a stage processor to retry. the system heals itself with no human intervention. poison pill quarantine - stop at the limit before resurrecting a job, the worker checks lease_recovery_count . if it has hit the configured limit (3), the job is not retried - the status is permanently set to failed and no outbox event is inserted. a payload that crashes the worker every single time is quarantined in the database. the rest of the infrastructure is protected. transactional integrity and session sweep the entire batch - resets, outbox inserts, and failure writes - happens inside one postgres transaction. if the database crashes mid-recovery, the transaction rolls back and the expired rows sit untouched until the next 60-second tick. before the transaction commits, the worker runs a session sweep - if any stage in a session was just permanently failed, the parent session status is updated to failed in the same commit. the state machine stays in sync.

items.snippet: Stage processor worker Firstly, why does the Stage Processor exist? The API’s job ends at 202 Accepted . It validated the data, locked the stage to PENDING , and wrote the outbox event. The actual KYC work - calling third-party verification services, analyzing documents, matching faces - has not happened yet. Something has to do it. That something cannot be the API. KYC checks are slow, unpredictable, and failure-prone. Doing them synchronously inside an HTTP request would mean the client waits minutes for a response , the API’s connection pool gets exhausted holding long-running requests open , and a single slow third-party service degrades the entire API for everyone else hitting it at the…
items.searchText: execution how the api and workers run: request flow, worker behavior, and end-to-end phases. execution stage processor worker  stage processor worker stage processor worker firstly, why does the stage processor exist? the api’s job ends at 202 accepted . it validated the data, locked the stage to pending , and wrote the outbox event. the actual kyc work - calling third-party verification services, analyzing documents, matching faces - has not happened yet. something has to do it. that something cannot be the api. kyc checks are slow, unpredictable, and failure-prone. doing them synchronously inside an http request would mean the client waits minutes for a response , the api’s connection pool gets exhausted holding long-running requests open , and a single slow third-party service degrades the entire api for everyone else hitting it at the same time . so the work is decoupled entirely. the api signals intent by writing to the outbox. the outbox relay picks that up and pushes a message to rabbitmq. the stage processor is the dedicated consumer of that queue - the process whose only job is to take that message, run the real verification work, write the result back to postgres, and drive the stage chain forward. it also owns the sequential unlock logic. when stage 1 finishes, stage 2 must start. there is no cron watching for that. the stage processor does it - in the same transaction that marks stage 1 completed , it inserts stage 2’s outbox event. the pipeline advances the instant the previous stage commits. no polling, no delay, no external scheduler. without the stage processor, the system has no execution engine. the data sits in pending forever and nothing ever moves. what it does and how it flows the muscle of the engine. consumes jobs from rabbitmq, runs the verification logic, drives the stage chain forward, and handles every failure path without losing data. intake - triage before anything else the worker is configured with qos prefetch of 1 (configurable) - rabbitmq only hands it one message at a time per thread. no memory floods from large backlogs. the moment a message arrives, it's triaged immediately. if the payload is missing event_id , session_id , or stage , the message is rejected with requeue: false and sent straight to the dead-letter queue. garbage is never retried. claim - idempotency and lease the worker tries to claim the stage by updating its status to processing and writing an expires_at 120 (configurable) seconds out. if postgres returns 0 rows updated, another worker already claimed it or it's already completed - the worker acks the rabbitmq message to clear it and moves on. no duplicate work, no errors. execution - concurrent jobs, isolated traces jobs run concurrently inside a joinset . each job gets its own tracing span so logs from 50 concurrent tasks never bleed into each other. a failure in job a produces a clean, isolated trace. stage handoff - decentralized orchestration when a stage completes, the worker checks inside the same transaction whether a successor stage is waiting. if it is, the outbox event for the next stage is inserted before the commit. stage 2 is triggered the instant stage 1 commits - no cron job, no external scheduler, no delay. session sweep - global state stays in sync after every completion or failure, the worker checks all five stages for the session. if all five are completed , the session is promoted to completed . if one is permanently failed , the session becomes failed . this happens in the same transaction as the stage update. failure path - retries then graveyard if the kyc work fails, the stage is set to failed and retry_count is incremented. if retries remain, the payload is published to the retry exchange - which holds it for 30 seconds before routing it back to the main queue - and the original message is acked. if retries are exhausted, the message is sent to the terminal dead-letter queue and the stage stays permanently failed . graceful shutdown - no mid-job corruption on sigterm , the worker stops pulling new messages immediately. it then waits for all in-flight jobs in the joinset to finish before the process exits. a deployment never kills a job halfway through.

items.searchText: execution how the api and workers run: request flow, worker behavior, and end-to-end phases. execution end-to-end walkthrough  end-to-end walkthrough end-to-end walkthrough preview - opens full diagram in google drive click to view end-to-end sequence diagram (google drive) phase 1 - session start ( post /session/start ) the api key is checked in constant time. if an idempotency key is present, redis is checked first - a duplicate returns the cached response immediately. otherwise, one transaction opens: one session row ( active ) and five stage rows ( locked ) are inserted together. transaction commits, result is cached in redis for 2 hours, 201 created returned with the session uuid. phase 2 - stage submission ( post /stages/{name} ) payload hits validatedjson - serde validates structure, validator enforces field rules, libphonenumber validates phone format where applicable. any failure returns 422 with field-level detail. inside a transaction, the stage is updated from locked to pending . zero rows affected means it was already submitted - 409 conflict returned, nothing else happens. then the dependency fork: independent stages (1, 4, 5) get an outbox_events row inserted immediately. dependent stages (2, 3) check if their predecessor is completed - if yes, outbox event is inserted; if no, the insert is skipped and the stage waits. transaction commits, 202 accepted returned. phase 3 - outbox relay every 500ms the relay fetches up to 50 rows using select for update skip locked from its own isolated pool. multiple relay pods grab different rows simultaneously, never blocking each other. each row is published to rabbitmq under a strict 50ms timeout. if the connection hangs, the timeout fires, the row is skipped, and the lock is released - database health over message speed. successfully acked message ids are collected, bulk-deleted from the outbox, and the transaction commits. phase 4 - stage processor worker consumes with prefetch of 1. malformed payload is immediately rejected to the dlq - no retries on garbage. valid payload opens a transaction, claims the stage ( processing ), and sets expires_at . success path: stage is updated to completed with response_data . if a successor stage is pending , its outbox event is inserted in the same transaction. session sweep runs - all five completed means the session becomes completed . transaction commits, rabbitmq ack sent. failure path: stage is updated to failed , error saved, retry_count incremented. if under the retry limit, the payload goes to the retry exchange (30s hold, then back to main queue) and the original message is acked. if retries are exhausted, the message is rejected to the terminal dead-letter queue and the stage stays permanently failed . session sweep runs in both cases. phase 5 - lease recovery every 60 seconds the worker queries for processing rows where expires_at < now() , in batches of 25 using skip locked . if lease_recovery_count is under the limit: stage reset to pending , count incremented, fresh outbox event inserted - the job re-enters the pipeline. if at the limit: stage permanently set to failed , no outbox event inserted, session sweep runs. a payload that crashes the worker every time is quarantined permanently. everything happens in one transaction - a database crash mid-recovery leaves the rows expired and untouched until the next tick. phase 6 - status poll ( get /status ) api key checked. two indexed queries run - one for the session row, one for all five stage rows. results mapped into sessionstatusresponse and returned as 200 ok . the core guarantee. the database is the source of truth. rabbitmq is purely a signal. the transactional outbox, skip locked concurrency, timed leases, and three-tier dead-letter topology together guarantee that every stage is eventually processed - regardless of network conditions, pod failures, or duplicate message delivery.

items.snippet: Indexing - performance decisions and tradeoffs Partial indexes over full indexes The core decision: only index rows that are actively being worked on. idx_stages_polling covers PENDING and PROCESSING rows only. idx_stages_processing_expires_at covers PROCESSING rows with a non-null expires_at only. The tradeoff is deliberate - historical COMPLETED rows, which will be the vast majority at scale, are excluded entirely. A standard index on status would grow with every verification ever processed, eventually becoming slow and memory-hungry. The performance gain - partial indexes stay small and fit in RAM regardless of how large the table grows. Worker query time stays flat instead of degrading a…
items.searchText: performance decisions indexing, ids, transactions, timeouts, retries, and failure handling tradeoffs and performance gains . performance decisions indexing - performance decisions and tradeoffs  indexing - performance decisions and tradeoffs indexing - performance decisions and tradeoffs partial indexes over full indexes the core decision: only index rows that are actively being worked on. idx_stages_polling covers pending and processing rows only. idx_stages_processing_expires_at covers processing rows with a non-null expires_at only. the tradeoff is deliberate - historical completed rows, which will be the vast majority at scale, are excluded entirely. a standard index on status would grow with every verification ever processed, eventually becoming slow and memory-hungry. the performance gain - partial indexes stay small and fit in ram regardless of how large the table grows. worker query time stays flat instead of degrading as data accumulates. ordered index on outbox_events performance gain idx_outbox_polling on created_at asc lets the relay grab the 50 oldest rows in arrival order without a sort operation. tradeoff - insert work pays a small overhead to maintain the index (negligible here because the table stays near-empty via deletion). skip locked only scales because the indexes are shaped correctly skip locked tells postgres to skip locked rows and return the next available ones. performance gain - with the right index, postgres finds the next available rows quickly; many worker pods grab disjoint batches without blocking each other. tradeoff if mis-shaped - without the aligned index, this degrades to row-by-row scan under load. core tradeoff in one sentence. slightly more complex index maintenance and a few extra bytes per write - in exchange, the performance gain : flat query time at any table size and contention-free concurrency across worker pods.

Performance gain - global uniqueness without coordination across API/worker pods or shards. items.searchText: performance decisions indexing, ids, transactions, timeouts, retries, and failure handling tradeoffs and performance gains . performance decisions uuidv4 - performance and security tradeoffs  uuidv4 - performance and security tradeoffs uuidv4 - performance and security tradeoffs what we gained performance gain - uuids are generated in memory with uuid::new_v4() ; no db round-trip. multi-stage transactions do not stall waiting for an id. sequential ids under concurrency trade off serial contention - every insert waits on a locked sequence. performance gain - global uniqueness without coordination across api/worker pods or shards. security gain - 122 bits of entropy makes the session id act as a bearer token; sequential ids make enumeration trivial. what we gave up tradeoff - 16 bytes per key vs 8, and more b-tree fragmentation than sequential keys because inserts land at random positions (more page splits, higher index maintenance). tradeoff in plain terms. extra disk and mild fragmentation - performance and ops gains : no sequence bottleneck, no cross-pod id coordination, idor resistance, safer sharding. for this workload, the tradeoff skews heavily toward uuids.

items.sectionTitle: Transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs
items.snippet: Transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs What SKIP LOCKED achieves in the outbox relay Firstly, without SKIP LOCKED , parallel workers become a single-file line at the database. Multiple workers polling the same table would block each other . Worker A locks a row; Worker B tries to read the same row and waits - held at the database level until Worker A releases the lock. Under high concurrency that turns into a queue of workers stacking up behind each other, serializing what should be parallel work . SKIP LOCKED flips that behavior. Instead of waiting, a worker skips rows that are already locked and moves to the next available one. Two workers polling at once get different row…
items.searchText: performance decisions indexing, ids, transactions, timeouts, retries, and failure handling tradeoffs and performance gains . performance decisions transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs  transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs transaction flows - performance decisions and tradeoffs what skip locked achieves in the outbox relay firstly, without skip locked , parallel workers become a single-file line at the database. multiple workers polling the same table would block each other . worker a locks a row; worker b tries to read the same row and waits - held at the database level until worker a releases the lock. under high concurrency that turns into a queue of workers stacking up behind each other, serializing what should be parallel work . skip locked flips that behavior. instead of waiting, a worker skips rows that are already locked and moves to the next available one. two workers polling at once get different rows with no coordination. ten workers get ten different batches. a hundred pods never touch the same row. the table acts as a self-distributing work queue - postgres partitions work through row-level locking alone. that is why both the outbox relay and the lease recovery worker scale horizontally. add pods and throughput tracks linearly: each pod works on rows no other pod is touching. the tradeoffs no ordering guarantee. skip locked does not promise insertion order. if worker a holds row 1, worker b skips it and takes row 2 - row 2 can finish first. for the outbox that is usually fine: the ordered index on created_at biases work toward oldest-first, but strict fifo is not enforced. at-least-once, not exactly-once. if a worker reads a row, publishes it, then crashes before deleting it, the next worker will pick the row up again. the design assumes that - idempotency keys on the consumer absorb duplicates. duplicates still happen; the system tolerates them, it does not erase them. locks for the whole transaction. while a worker is publishing to rabbitmq it still holds the postgres row lock. a slow or hung publish keeps those rows unavailable to every other worker until the transaction ends. that is why the 50ms publish timeout exists - it caps how long a stalled connection can hoard locks. invisible rows under heavy contention. if every row in a candidate batch is already locked, skip locked returns an empty set instead of waiting. a worker may poll, find nothing, and sleep even though work still sits in the table - momentarily claimed by others. expected behavior, not a bug; under extreme contention throughput is not perfectly linear. session creation - one transaction, bulk insert the session row and all five stage rows are written in one transaction using unnest (one sql statement vs five round-trips). performance gain - one network round-trip to postgres regardless of stage count. tradeoff - slightly larger write transaction; lock time stays small, and latency drops materially . stage submission - conditional outbox inside one transaction the locked → pending update, dependency check, and outbox insert share one transaction scope - no second connection, no race between read and insert. tradeoff - transaction stays open slightly longer for the conditional read. performance gain - removes dual-write bugs where the stage updates but the outbox row never lands. outbox relay - row locks held only as long as publish takes the relay locks up to 50 rows, publishes, deletes only acked rows, commits; per-row publish timeout caps lock duration. tradeoff - slow rabbitmq extends lock hold time up to the timeout cap. performance gain - timeouts prevent unbounded lock hoarding; timed-out rows retry next cycle without blocking the batch indefinitely. stage claim - atomic pending → processing with lease single update claims the stage and sets expires_at ; zero rows means another worker won. tradeoff - one round-trip per message. performance gain - no check-then-act gap where two workers could claim the same stage. stage completion and successor unlock - same transaction completing a stage and enqueueing the successor outbox row are atomic. tradeoff - slightly longer transaction. performance gain - sequencing enforced in the database without orchestrator, scheduler, or inter-stage delay; rollback never queues a successor by mistake. session sweep - batch update, single round-trip session sweep uses = any($1) over a batch of session ids. performance gain - o(1) statements instead of n round-trips for n sessions; sweep cost stays flat with batch size. tradeoff - three conditional updates per call (mutually exclusive targets, so no double-writes). lease recovery - cte-based single-statement update expired rows move to pending or failed in one cte; lock acquisition and write share one execution. tradeoff - more complex sql to maintain. performance gain - no window where two recovery workers claim the same expired row.

items.snippet: Edge security API key authentication uses constant-time comparison, not standard string equality. Standard comparison fails the moment it hits a wrong character - measurable response time differences let attackers guess keys byte by byte. Constant-time comparison takes identical time regardless of where the mismatch occurs, eliminating the attack vector entirely. Idempotency keys backed by Redis mean duplicate session-start requests - whether from a glitchy network or a deliberate flood - return the cached response. The database sees exactly one write regardless of how many requests arrive. Rate limits and CORS origins are environment variables. Under an active attack, both can be tightened …

items.snippet: Payload defense ValidatedJson enforces a two-phase gate before any handler runs. Serde validates structure first - wrong types are rejected. The validator crate then enforces field rules: string length caps prevent memory exhaustion, strict enums reject anything outside the allowed set. Phone numbers go through libphonenumber - regex is not sufficient for international formats. If a crafted payload crashes the worker, the lease recovery system tracks it. After three crashes, the payload is permanently quarantined as FAILED and removed from active processing. The cluster is protected from infinite crash loops.

items.snippet: Database security UUID primary keys carry 122 bits of entropy - sequential IDs make enumeration trivial, UUIDs make it computationally impossible. No user_id is stored anywhere in the verification tables. If the database is breached, the attacker gets fragmented verification data with no way to link it to real user accounts. The session UUID is the only credential. All SQL uses parameterized queries verified at compile time against the actual schema. SQL injection is not possible - there is no dynamic query construction. input_data is written once by the API, response_data written once by the worker. Neither can be modified after the fact. The audit trail is structurally immutable.

items.snippet: Container hardening Production images are built on distroless base - no shell, no package manager, no system utilities. Remote code execution via a zero-day lands an attacker in a container with nothing to work with. The Rust binary runs as PID 1 directly. Database migrations run in a separate short-lived Alpine container that exits immediately after applying the schema. The API and worker pods have no schema-altering privileges and no migration code.

items.snippet: Leak prevention All secrets - database URL, Redis URL, AMQP URL, API key - are wrapped in secrecy::SecretString . Accidentally printing the config struct outputs [REDACTED] . Secrets cannot reach logs regardless of developer error. Internal 500 errors log the full technical detail internally. The client receives only {"error": "internal_error", "message": "An unexpected error occurred", "trace_id": "..."} . Database schema names, table structures, and stack traces never leave the server.

items.sectionTitle: Foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes
items.snippet: Foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes The test suite is divided into three completely separate environments. Each exists for a different reason and tests a different level of the system. Unit tests run entirely in memory. No database, no Redis, no RabbitMQ. They test pure logic - config parsing, error mapping, validation boundaries, type serialization. If a unit test needs infrastructure to run, it's testing the wrong thing. Integration tests run against real infrastructure. Every test gets its own ephemeral Postgres database, spun up and torn down automatically. They test that the actual SQL, the actual locks, the actual business rules, and the actual HTTP layer all behave correc…
items.searchText: testing unit, integration, and stress tests - three worlds, three purposes. testing foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes  foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes foundation: three testing worlds, three purposes the test suite is divided into three completely separate environments. each exists for a different reason and tests a different level of the system. unit tests run entirely in memory. no database, no redis, no rabbitmq. they test pure logic - config parsing, error mapping, validation boundaries, type serialization. if a unit test needs infrastructure to run, it's testing the wrong thing. integration tests run against real infrastructure. every test gets its own ephemeral postgres database, spun up and torn down automatically. they test that the actual sql, the actual locks, the actual business rules, and the actual http layer all behave correctly when wired together. mocks are deliberately avoided here - the point is to prove the system works against real postgres transactions and real redis state. stress tests run against the full live cluster - api, worker, postgres, redis, rabbitmq all running simultaneously. they don't test correctness; they test behavior under load. the goal is to find where the system bends before it breaks in production.

items.snippet: Stress tests - full cluster under load Every verification session is launched at the exact same millisecond using Promise.allSettled - a deliberate thundering herd. This forces Postgres row locks, outbox micro-batching, and RabbitMQ routing to prove themselves under maximum contention simultaneously. The script mounts the Docker socket and acts as its own infrastructure monitor - querying real-time CPU, RAM, and network I/O for every container every 3 seconds. Network stats are delta-computed so the numbers reflect bandwidth during the test window specifically, not since the container booted. items.searchText: testing unit, integration, and stress tests - three worlds, three purposes. testing stress tests - full cluster under load  stress tests - full cluster under load stress tests - full cluster under load every verification session is launched at the exact same millisecond using promise.allsettled - a deliberate thundering herd. this forces postgres row locks, outbox micro-batching, and rabbitmq routing to prove themselves under maximum contention simultaneously. the script mounts the docker socket and acts as its own infrastructure monitor - querying real-time cpu, ram, and network i/o for every container every 3 seconds. network stats are delta-computed so the numbers reflect bandwidth during the test window specifically, not since the container booted. a slow response can be traced directly to a container hitting 100% cpu. results are written to three csv files: session-level outcomes - pass/fail result for every verification session in the run. per-stage round-trip and wall-clock timing - granular latency for each of the five stages across all sessions. hardware telemetry - cpu, ram, and network i/o mapped against elapsed test seconds for every container. internal status codes are translated to plain english before writing - locked becomes "not started yet" - so results are readable without understanding the state machine. a hard deadline backed by an abortcontroller guarantees the script exits cleanly even during a catastrophic cluster failure. whatever data was collected up to that point is written to csv. test data is never lost. node.js was chosen over rust for the stress script - native fetch , async event loop, and json/csv handling without compilation overhead. it's the right tool for firing concurrent http traffic and wrangling reports.

items.snippet: Boot setup Both the API and worker call one initialization function at startup. Log level is controlled by RUST_LOG - defaults to info in production to keep log volume and cost low. Injecting RUST_LOG=info,app_core=debug at runtime enables deep traces for a specific crate without redeployment. Log format switches on environment. Local gets human-readable output with file names and line numbers - click a log line, jump to the code. Production gets compact JSON - lower CPU overhead, easy for log aggregators to parse.

items.snippet: Spans over log lines Everything is structured around spans, not individual log statements. A span covers a duration and carries context - session_id , stage , outbox_id . Every log event emitted inside a span automatically inherits that context. You don't pass variables through every function call; the span carries them.

items.snippet: trace_id on every API error Tower's trace middleware stamps a unique 16-character hex ID on every HTTP request. When the API returns a 4xx or 5xx, that ID is injected directly into the JSON error response. A customer pastes that ID into the log search and the exact database query, validation failure, and stack trace for their specific request appears instantly. No reproduction steps needed.

items.snippet: Worker spans built manually Workers have no incoming HTTP request, so spans are constructed explicitly. The outbox relay extracts session_id and stage from the payload before publishing and attaches them to the span - a failed publish log reads "failed to publish Stage 2 for Session XYZ", not just "publish failed." The stage processor uses .instrument(span) on each async task in the JoinSet . Standard span entry would bleed log context across concurrently running jobs sharing the same thread. .instrument() binds the span to the specific future, not the thread - Job A's logs never mix with Job B's.

items.snippet: Surgical field recording Instead of logging generic completion messages, specific counts are recorded directly onto the span - recovered jobs, permanently failed jobs. Log aggregators can graph these fields in real time without custom parsing rules.

items.snippet: Debug gate Expensive log operations - like printing a list of all published outbox IDs - are gated behind tracing::enabled!(Level::DEBUG) . At info level, that code path is never evaluated. Debug detail is available when needed without paying the cost in production.
